en.extract:
Mrs. Gould's sunbird (Aethopyga gouldiae) is a sunbird species native to forests and shrublands from the southern foothills of the Himalayas to Southeast Asia.


Taxonomy
Mrs. Gould's sunbird was formally described in 1831 as Cinnyris gouldiae by the Irish zoologist Nicholas Vigors based on a specimen that John Gould had received from India. The specific epithet was chosen to honour the bird artist Elizabeth Gould who had drawn a plate of the bird for publication in the book A Century of Birds from the Himalaya Mountains. Vigors specified the type locality as the Himalayas but this was restricted to the Silma-Almora district (Almora to Shimla in India) by Claud Ticehurst and Hugh Whistler in 1924. Mrs. Gould's sunbird is now one of 21 species placed in the genus Aethopyga that was introduced in 1851 by the German ornithologist Jean Cabanis.
Four subspecies are recognised:

A. g. gouldiae (Vigors, NA, 1831) – Himalayas (Sutlej Valley to Arunachal Pradesh and southeastern Tibet)
A. g. isolata Baker, ECS, 1925 – southern Assam to Bangladesh and western Myanmar (Chin Hills)
A. g. dabryii (Verreaux, JP, 1867) – western China (Xinjiang to Sichuan and Yunnan) to Myanmar and northern Laos
A. g. annamensis Robinson, HC & Kloss, CB, 1919 – southern Laos (Bolaven Plateau) and southern Vietnam (Langbian Plateau)
The species is regarded as closely related to the green-tailed sunbird.


Description
Mrs. Gould's sunbird is a small sunbird. It has a down-curved and pointed beak, typical for a nectar feeder. The iris of the eye is usually deep brown, and the tarsus is black.
The male Mrs. Gould's sunbird is bright and colourful. The forehead to crown, supercilium and throat of the sunbird are deep violet. The lore, the auriculars and the malar region, the nape, mantle and side are bright red to deep scarlet. Bright blue patterns may be present on the auricular and the side. The coverts and wingbars to the primary feathers are brown or olive green. The belly and vent are yellowish-green. The tail covert is bright blue and fades to dark purple at the tip of the tail.
The female is dull in colour compared to the male. The female is covered by deep olive green on the dorsal side and greyish-yellow on the ventral side.
The colour and pattern may be different in different subspecies. For example, the breast of A. g. dabryii is purely scarlet, and A. g. gouldiae has a bright yellow breast, with or without a red stripe.
The male weighs 4–12 g and measures 131–160 mm; wing length is 51–58 mm, tail length is 64–88mm, and tarsus length is 13–15.5 mm. The female weighs 5–8 g and measures 91–111 mm; wing length is 45–54 mm, tail length is 30.5–40 mm, and tarsus length is 12–16 mm. Both sexes have a beak 13–17 mm in length.


Distribution and habitat
Mrs. Gould's sunbird is widely distributed in Eastern Bangladesh, Bhutan, Laos, Myanmar, Nepal, Northern Thailand, India (from Jammu to Arunachal Pradesh), Pakistan (Margalla Hills), Vietnam and Southern China. It is found in the evergreen broad-leaved forest, monsoon forest and deciduous broad-leaved forest at 1000–3500 m above sea-level. It is also occasionally found in orchards and bamboo forest within its range. There is a non-breeding population in Hong Kong.


Behaviour and ecology

Mrs. Gould's sunbird is generally resident but may undertake small-scale seasonal migrations. It is usually seen solitary or in pairs although temporary groups of 3–5 or even more than 10 individuals can form. It moves quickly and flies for short distances. Calls include a high thin "tzeeee" and sharp "tzit".


Food and feeding
As a typical sunbird, Mrs. Gould's sunbird feeds on nectar. The elongated and tubular tongue is modified for sucking the nectar from tube-shape flowers. It also takes small invertebrates. Diet in Sichuan and Yunnan was found to include spiders, small beetles and hemipteran nymphs.


Breeding
The breeding period of Mrs. Gould's sunbird is April to June. Females nest around mid-April. The nest is built of moss, grass, plant fibres and spiderwebs. It is 15–18 cm long and 10–11.5 cm wide, and usually built in deciduous broad-leaved forest at 1000–3000m above sea level. In the breeding season, the males court the females with a mating call "zhai-zhai-zhai". Clutch size is 2–3 eggs. The eggs are white and usually have reddish brown spots. Incubation lasts for about two weeks, and chicks fledge after 15–16 days. In this period, both parents care for the chicks. The adults feed nectar to the young chicks by regurgitation at the beginning; later on invertebrates are increasingly provided.


Status and conservation
The species is listed as Least Concern in the IUCN Red List. It is not on the list of endangered and protected species of China. However, it is on the List of Wild Animals Protected by the PRC that Are Beneficial or of High Value to Economy and Science.


References

[[File:MRS GOULD'S-SUNBIRD.jpg|thumb|'''Mrs. Gould's sunbird''' ('''''Aethopyga gouldiae''''') clicked at Mandala, Arunachal Pradesh]]
'''Mrs. Gould's sunbird''' ('''''Aethopyga gouldiae''''') is a [[sunbird]] species native to forests and shrublands from the southern foothills of the [[Himalayas]] to [[Southeast Asia]].

== Taxonomy ==
Mrs. Gould's sunbird was [[species description|formally described]] in 1831 as ''Cinnyris gouldiae'' by the Irish zoologist [[Nicholas Aylward Vigors|Nicholas Vigors]] based on a specimen that [[John Gould]] had received from India. The specific epithet was chosen to honour the bird artist [[Elizabeth Gould (illustrator)|Elizabeth Gould]] who had drawn a plate of the bird for publication in the book ''[[A Century of Birds from the Himalaya Mountains]]''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Vigors | first=Nicholas Aylward | author-link=Nicholas Aylward Vigors | year=1831 | title=The Chairman again resumed the exhibition of the Himalayan birds | journal=Proceedings of the Committee of Science and Correspondence of the Zoological Society of London | volume=1 | pages=41-44 [44] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/12861452 }} Himalayan birds are described on pages 7-9, 22-24, 35, 41-44, 54-56, 170-176 in the same volume.</ref><ref>{{ cite book | last=Vigors | first=Nicholas Aylward | author-link=Nicholas Aylward Vigors | editor-last1=Gould | editor1-first=John | editor1-link=John Gould | editor2-last=Gould | editor2-first=Elizabeth | editor2-link=Elizabeth Gould (illustrator) | date=1831 |title=Century of Birds from the Himalaya Mountains | location=London | publisher=John Gould | at=Plate 56 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/42203678 }} For the date and publication details see: {{Cite journal | last1=McAllen | first1=I. a. W. | last2=Bruce | first2=M. D. | date=2002 | title=Systematic notes on Asian birds. 27. On the dates of publication of John Gould’s 'A Century of Birds from the Himalaya Mountains' | journal=Zoologische Verhandelingen | volume=340 | pages=161-177 | url=https://repository.naturalis.nl/pub/220207}}</ref> Vigors specified the [[type locality (biology)|type locality]] as the Himalayas but this was restricted to the Silma-Almora district ([[Almora]] to [[Shimla]] in India) by [[Claud B. Ticehurst|Claud Ticehurst]] and [[Hugh Whistler]] in 1924.<ref>{{ cite journal | last1=Ticehurst | first1=C.B. | author1-link=Claud B. Ticehurst | last2=Whistler | first2=H. | author2-link=Hugh Whistler | date=1924 | title=On the type-locality of certain birds described by Vigors | journal=Ibis | volume=66 | issue=3 | pages=468-473 | doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1924.tb05337.x }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Paynter | editor-first=Raymond A. Jr | year=1986 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=12 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | location=Cambridge, Massachusetts | pages=273-274 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14482390 }}</ref> Mrs. Gould's sunbird is now one of 21 species placed in the genus ''[[Aethopyga]]'' that was introduced in 1851 by the German ornithologist [[Jean Cabanis]].<ref name=avilist/>

Four [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=avilist>{{ cite web | author=AviList Core Team | date=2025 | title=AviList: The Global Avian Checklist, v2025 | doi=10.2173/avilist.v2025 | doi-access=free | url=http://www.avilist.org/checklist/v2025/ | access-date=31 January 2026 }}</ref>
* ''A. g. gouldiae'' ([[Nicholas Aylward Vigors|Vigors, NA]], 1831) – Himalayas ([[Sutlej|Sutlej Valley]] to [[Arunachal Pradesh]] and southeastern Tibet)
* ''A. g. isolata'' [[E. C. Stuart Baker|Baker, ECS]], 1925 – southern [[Assam]] to Bangladesh and western Myanmar ([[Chin Hills]])
* ''A. g. dabryii'' ([[Jules Verreaux|Verreaux, JP]], 1867) – western China ([[Xinjiang]] to [[Sichuan]] and [[Yunnan]]) to Myanmar and northern Laos
* ''A. g. annamensis'' [[Herbert C. Robinson|Robinson, HC]] & [[C. Boden Kloss|Kloss, CB]], 1919 – southern Laos ([[Bolaven Plateau]]) and southern Vietnam ([[Đà Lạt Plateau|Langbian Plateau]])

The species is regarded as closely related to the [[green-tailed sunbird]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Wang|first1=Ning|last2=Liang|first2=Bin|date=2016-07-03|title=Complete mitochondrial genome of a sunbird, Aethopyga gouldiae (Aves: Passeriformes), the first representative of Nectariniidae|journal=Mitochondrial DNA Part A|volume=27|issue=4|pages=2356–2358|doi=10.3109/19401736.2015.1025262|issn=2470-1394|pmid=25990041|s2cid=207579660}}</ref>
[[File:Mrs. Gould's-Sunbird.jpg|thumb|Mrs. Gould's sunbird: male and female together]]

==Description==
Mrs. Gould's sunbird is a small [[sunbird]]. It has a down-curved and pointed beak, typical for a [[nectar feeder]]. The iris of the eye is usually deep brown, and the tarsus is black.

The male Mrs. Gould's sunbird is bright and colourful. The forehead to crown, supercilium and throat of the sunbird are deep violet. The lore, the auriculars and the malar region, the nape, mantle and side are bright red to deep scarlet. Bright blue patterns may be present on the auricular and the side. The coverts and wingbars to the primary feathers are brown or olive green. The belly and vent are yellowish-green. The tail covert is bright blue and fades to dark purple at the tip of the tail.

The female is dull in colour compared to the male. The female is covered by deep olive green on the dorsal side and greyish-yellow on the ventral side.

The colour and pattern may be different in different subspecies. For example, the breast of ''A. g. dabryii'' is purely scarlet, and ''A. g. gouldiae'' has a bright yellow breast, with or without a red stripe.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=赵正阶|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=AR_hSgAACAAJ|title=中国鸟类志: 雀形目. 下册|date=2001|publisher=吉林科学技术出版社|isbn=978-7-5384-2407-2|language=zh}}</ref>

The male weighs 4–12 g and measures 131–160 mm; wing length is 51–58 mm, tail length is 64–88mm, and tarsus length is 13–15.5 mm. The female weighs 5–8 g and measures 91–111 mm; wing length is 45–54 mm, tail length is 30.5–40 mm, and tarsus length is 12–16 mm. Both sexes have a beak 13–17 mm in length.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=中国动物志: 鸟纲. 第十三卷. 雀形目(山雀科-绣眼鸟科)|url=http://book.sciencereading.cn/shop/book/Booksimple/show.do?id=BC90BB7458D5748DC892FF297795E8F17000|access-date=2020-10-13|website=book.sciencereading.cn}}</ref>

== Distribution and habitat ==
Mrs. Gould's sunbird is widely distributed in Eastern [[Bangladesh]], [[Bhutan]], [[Laos]], [[Myanmar]], [[Nepal]], Northern [[Thailand]], [[India]] (from [[Jammu division|Jammu]] to [[Arunachal Pradesh]]), [[Pakistan]] ([[Margalla Hills]]), [[Vietnam]] and Southern [[China]].<ref name="iucn status 15 November 2021" /><ref name=":1" /><ref>{{Cite book |last=Grimmett, Roberts Inskipp |title=Birds of Pakistan |publisher=[[Christopher Helm]] |isbn=9780713688009 |location=London |publication-date=2009}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Mrs. Gould's Sunbird - eBird |url=https://ebird.org/species/gousun1 |archive-url=http://web.archive.org/web/20260129212831/https://ebird.org/species/gousun1 |archive-date=2026-01-29 |access-date=2026-03-07 |website=ebird.org |language=en}}</ref> It is found in the evergreen broad-leaved forest, monsoon forest and deciduous broad-leaved forest at 1000–3500 m above sea-level. It is also occasionally found in orchards and bamboo forest within its range. There is a non-breeding population in [[Hong Kong]].<ref name=":1" />

==Behaviour and ecology==
[[File:Elizabeth Gould's 1831 illustration of Mrs. Gould's sunbird.png|thumb|Elizabeth Gould's 1831 illustration of her namesake bird]]
Mrs. Gould's sunbird is generally resident but may undertake small-scale seasonal migrations.<ref>{{Cite web|title=蓝喉太阳鸟 - 中国自然保护区生物标本资源共享平台|url=http://www.papc.cn/html/report/1849062-1.htm|access-date=2020-10-13|website=www.papc.cn}}</ref> It is usually seen solitary or in pairs although temporary groups of 3–5 or even more than 10 individuals can form. It moves quickly and flies for short distances. Calls include a high thin "tzeeee" and sharp "tzit".<ref>{{Cite web|title=Mrs. Gould's Sunbird - eBird|url=https://ebird.org/species/gousun1|access-date=2020-10-13|website=ebird.org|language=en}}</ref>

=== Food and feeding ===
As a typical sunbird, Mrs. Gould's sunbird feeds on nectar. The elongated and tubular tongue is modified for sucking the nectar from tube-shape flowers. It also takes small invertebrates. Diet in Sichuan and Yunnan was found to include spiders, small beetles and [[hemiptera]]n nymphs.<ref name=":1" />

=== Breeding ===
The breeding period of Mrs. Gould's sunbird is April to June. Females nest around mid-April. The nest is built of moss, grass, plant fibres and spiderwebs. It is 15–18 cm long and 10–11.5 cm wide, and usually built in deciduous broad-leaved forest at 1000–3000m above sea level. In the breeding season, the males court the females with a mating call "zhai-zhai-zhai".<ref name=":0" /> Clutch size is 2–3 eggs. The eggs are white and usually have reddish brown spots. Incubation lasts for about two weeks, and chicks fledge after 15–16 days. In this period, both parents care for the chicks. The adults feed nectar to the young chicks by [[Regurgitation (digestion)|regurgitation]] at the beginning; later on invertebrates are increasingly provided.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=刘九江|date=2017|title=蓝喉太阳鸟|url=http://www.cqvip.com/QK/85652A/201702/671470238.html|journal=林业与生态|issue=2|page=45}}</ref>

== Status and conservation ==
The species is listed as [[Least Concern]] in the [[IUCN Red List]].<ref name="iucn status 15 November 2021" /> It is not on the list of endangered and protected species of China. However, it is on the List of Wild Animals Protected by the PRC that Are Beneficial or of High Value to Economy and Science.<ref>{{Cite web|last=|first=|date=2020-10-13|title=List of Wild Animal that Protected by PRC with Beneficial and High Value of Economy and Science|url=http://www.forestry.gov.cn/portal/bhxh/s/709/content-466654.html|website=Ministry of Natural Resource of the People's Republic of China}}</ref>

zh.extract:
蓝喉太阳鸟（学名：Aethopyga gouldiae）为太阳鸟科太阳鸟属的鸟类，俗名桐花凤。分布于印度、东至中南半岛以及中国大陆的西藏、四川、云南、贵州、陕西、广西、湖北、湖南等地，主要生活于海拔1000-3100米的高原盆地和山地的阔叶林、沟谷林、稀树灌丛以至河边和公路边的乔木树丛和竹丛中以及尤喜在花丛间活动。该物种的模式产地在喜马拉雅山脉地区。


亚种
蓝喉太阳鸟西南亚种（学名：Aethopyga gouldiae dabryii）。分布于自印度以东至泰国以北的中南半岛以及中国大陆的甘肃、四川、陕西、云南、贵州、广西、湖北、湖南等地。该物种的模式产地在四川康定,据Slater。
蓝喉太阳鸟指名亚种（学名：Aethopyga gouldiae gouldiae）。分布于印度以及中国大陆的西藏等地。该物种的模式产地在喜马拉雅山脉地区。
